q: Increasing the concentrations of the reactants increases the rate of reactions for many reactions involving liquids or gases.
If more molecules or ions of the reactants are in the reaction area, then there is a greater chance of more effective collisions per unit time.
Zinc granule reacts much faster with concentrated $6.0\ mol\ dm^{-3}$ hydrochloric acid than with $1.0\ mol\ dm^{-3}$ dilute hydrochloric acid.
In reactions involving solids, a reaction occurs at the boundary surface between reactants.
The rate of a reaction involving a solid reactant is decreased by increasing the surface area of the solid.
The smaller the particle size, the larger the surface area will be.
If the surface area is larger, it will be hit by more moving reacting particles per unit time, and the rate of reaction will be faster.
Aluminium foil reacts moderately with sodium hydroxide solution only when warmed.
Powdered aluminium of the same mass reacts rapidly in the cold with sodium hydroxide solution.
Large pieces of wood are difficult to ignite whereas small pieces burn more rapidly.
Changing the pressure does not affect the rate of reaction that only involves solids or liquids.
Increasing the pressure increases the speed of a reaction involving gases.
The pressure is directly proportional to the volume of a gas at a constant temperature.
At low pressure, the volume becomes larger, the number of collisions of gaseous molecules is smaller, and thus, the rate of reaction is slower.
At high pressure, the volume becomes smaller, the number of collisions is greater, and thus, the rate of reaction is faster.
Food can cook faster in a pressure cooker than in a pot because trapped steam becomes hotter as the pressure increases.
Most chemical reactions react faster at a low temperature than at a high temperature.
The kinetic energy of a gas is directly proportional to the temperature.
Increasing the temperature increases the kinetic energy, which means molecules move faster and collide more frequently with sufficient activation energy.
The number of effective collisions per second of the reacting particles increases as temperature increases.
Foods are cooled or frozen to speed up the chemical reactions that result in the spoiling of food.
The effect of temperature on reaction rates can also be explained by using the Boltzmann distribution curve.
The area under the Boltzmann distribution curve represents the number of particles within a certain energy range.
Chemical reactions take place only if the collided particles possess activation energy, $E_a$.
When the temperature is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$, the shape of the distribution curve remains unchanged.
Generally, for a ten degrees rise in temperature, the rate of reactions approximately doubles.
A catalyst is defined as a substance which alters the rate of a chemical reaction but remains chemically changed at the end of the reaction.
Positive catalysts make chemical reactions go faster.
Iron is used as a positive catalyst in the Haber Process for manufacturing ammonia.
Vanadium(V) oxide or platinised asbestos is used in the Contact Process for manufacturing sulphur trioxide.
Manganese(IV) oxide is used in the preparation of oxygen from hydrogen peroxide.
A negative catalyst is used when a chemical reaction needs to go more slowly or to suppress unwanted reactions.
Alcohol acts as a negative catalyst to slow down the oxidation of sodium sulphite to sodium sulphate.
Catalysts provide an alternative pathway for the reaction with lower activation energy.
The rate of catalysed reaction is faster than that of uncatalysed reaction.
Homogeneous catalysts are in a different phase from the reactants in a catalysed reaction.
Heterogeneous catalysts are in a different phase from the reactants in a catalysed reaction.
Most heterogeneous catalysts are solids that catalyse either gaseous or liquid reactants.
In the ammonia oxidation process (Ostwald process), Pt-Rh catalyst speeds up the reaction.
Enzymes act as biocatalysts for specific biochemical reactions in living systems.
Ptyalin in saliva and pepsin in gastric juice are digestive enzymes that hasten the breakdown of large molecules.
The reaction rate using enzymes is much slower than inorganic catalysts.
Reactions that are initiated by light are called photochemical reactions.
The brighter the light, the faster the photochemical reaction.
Photosynthesis in green plants is an example of a photochemical reaction.
Decomposition of silver bromide in photographic films is not a photochemical reaction.
The reaction between hydrogen and chlorine is extremely slow in the dark but becomes explosive in intense sunlight via a chain reaction.
Phase is a portion of a physical state that is homogeneous throughout and can be separated physically from other phases.
When large amounts of flammable powdered substances collect in grain elevators or coal mines, violent explosions can occur.
Storage of preserved foods at a sub-zero temperature in a refrigerator is effective for slowing the rate of bacterial decay.
At high altitudes where the temperature is less than $100\ ^{\circ}C$, water boils at $100\ ^{\circ}C$.
Corrosion inhibitors like imidazoline and quaternary amine prevent $H_2S$ and $CO_2$ corrosion.
Volatile corrosion inhibitors (VCI) such as morpholine or hydrazine prevent corrosion in boilers.
Wrapping metals in volatile corrosion inhibitor-coated paper is a common long-term protection against moisture.
